skill.name: openclaw-secure-linux-cloud
skill.description: Guide secure self-hosting of OpenClaw on Linux cloud servers with private-first access, host hardening, Podman deployment, token authentication, pairing, sandboxing, and cautious tool permissions.
skill.summary: Secure OpenClaw cloud deployment guidance for Linux hosts using loopback access, SSH tunnels, Podman, and hardened defaults.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified many privileged command, network, and filesystem patterns, but the scanned files are documentation for secure OpenClaw deployment rather than executable code. The confirmed risk is operational: users may run sudo commands, modify firewall and SSH settings, enable services, or expose secrets while following examples. No evidence found of prompt injection, covert exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Linux Administration Commands
medium_findings.description: The reference includes sudo commands that update packages, change SSH configuration, write firewall rules, enable services, clone software into /opt, and restart OpenClaw services. These are legitimate for this security-hardening skill, but they can lock out users, alter host security boundaries, or install services if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly privileged host-modification examples. The surrounding context frames them as hardening guidance, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive OpenClaw Token and Config File Handling
medium_findings.description: The reference shows reading OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN from an .env file and changing permissions on the OpenClaw configuration directory. This is expected for deployment guidance, but it involves secrets and should be handled carefully to avoid disclosure in terminals, logs, or shared chat transcripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly references a gateway token in a hidden .env path and config permissions. It is semantically relevant and not hidden, but it remains sensitive handling guidance.

medium_findings.title: Service Enablement and Persistence Instructions
medium_findings.description: The reference enables unattended-upgrades and nftables, then starts or restarts OpenClaw-related user services. This persistence is normal for server deployment, but marketplace users should understand these commands change long-running host behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The systemd and service-management examples are clear. They are documented deployment steps, not covert persistence.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Are Not Auto-Executed
low_findings.description: Many external-command findings are shell snippets inside markdown documentation. The skill does not include executable scripts or hooks that automatically run these commands; users must choose to run them manually.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown documentation and fenced command examples. No executable source file or automatic runner is present in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network Values Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL cites the source article, while 127.0.0.1 and 0.0.0.0 are used to explain loopback binding and unsafe public binding. I did not find evidence of data exfiltration or unauthorized network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network values are embedded in explanatory text and local tunnel examples. Their context is defensive guidance, not outbound collection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography, but the relevant text recommends ed25519 SSH keys or mentions packages and stronger authentication. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or custom weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain documentation text and package names, not weak algorithm use. The static labels appear to be keyword matches without semantic confirmation.

content.value_statement: Self-hosted OpenClaw can expose a powerful control plane if cloud access is configured too broadly. This skill guides a private-first Linux deployment with hardened host settings, loopback binding, SSH tunnels, Podman, token authentication, pairing, and sandbox defaults.
actual_capabilities: Creates private-first OpenClaw deployment guidance for Linux cloud hosts.
Separates local machine steps from server-side hardening tasks.
Compares SSH tunnel, Tailscale, and reverse-proxy access models.
Provides Debian and Ubuntu example commands for host hardening.
Defines a conservative OpenClaw configuration baseline.
Builds pre-launch and day-two security review checklists.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not deploy or audit a host automatically.
The concrete package examples are centered on Debian and Ubuntu systems.
OpenClaw commands and configuration keys may change after the referenced guidance.
Users must review privileged commands before applying them to production servers.

use_cases.title: Plan a First Private Deployment
use_cases.description: Design an OpenClaw cloud setup that keeps the gateway on loopback and reaches the Control UI through SSH.

use_cases.title: Review Existing Exposure
use_cases.description: Check whether an existing OpenClaw host exposes the gateway, weakens token authentication, or grants broad tool access.

use_cases.title: Choose a Remote Access Model
use_cases.description: Compare SSH tunneling, Tailscale, and reverse proxy options before widening access beyond a single administrator.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Secure Baseline
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a secure OpenClaw deployment on a new Ubuntu cloud server. Keep the Control UI private and separate local steps from server steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Current Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my OpenClaw server design for risky exposure. Focus on gateway binding, firewall rules, token authentication, pairing, sandboxing, and tool permissions.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Access Paths
prompt_templates.prompt: I need remote OpenClaw access from trusted devices. Compare SSH tunnel, Tailscale, and reverse proxy options, then recommend the lowest-risk path.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Production Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production readiness checklist for an OpenClaw Linux cloud host. Include host hardening, service checks, secret handling, sandbox policy, and day-two audits.

output_examples.input: I want to run OpenClaw on a small cloud VM for personal use.
output: Recommended architecture: keep the gateway private on loopback and reach the Control UI through an SSH tunnel.
Server work: harden SSH, apply a default-deny firewall, install rootless Podman, and keep OpenClaw service permissions narrow.
Local work: generate or use an SSH key, connect to the server, then open a local tunnel only when you need the UI.

output_examples.input: Should I put OpenClaw behind a reverse proxy?
output: Default recommendation: do not start with public exposure.
Use an SSH tunnel for initial deployment. Use Tailscale when trusted devices need repeated access.
Choose a reverse proxy only when public access is required and stronger monitoring, authentication, and boundary controls are in place.

output_examples.input: What should I check before launch?
output: Confirm SSH key login works and root password login is disabled.
Confirm the firewall exposes only intended services and the OpenClaw UI is not public.
Confirm token authentication, pairing, narrow tool defaults, sandboxing, and tight config permissions are enabled.

best_practices: Keep the OpenClaw gateway private until a clear access requirement justifies broader exposure.
Review every privileged command against the target distribution before running it.
Recheck service status, firewall exposure, secrets, and sandbox policy after each configuration change.
anti_patterns: Do not expose the Control UI publicly just because token authentication is enabled.
Do not disable pairing, sandboxing, or narrow tool defaults to speed up initial setup.
Do not paste gateway tokens or .env output into shared tickets, logs, or chat transcripts.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium risk warning. It contains privileged administration examples, but no executable automation or malicious intent was found.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The scanned files are markdown guidance. Users must manually decide whether to run any command examples.

faq.question: Which operating systems does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Linux cloud hosts. Debian and Ubuntu examples are the clearest path in the reference material.

faq.question: Why does it prefer SSH tunneling?
faq.answer: SSH tunneling keeps the OpenClaw Control UI private and reduces public attack surface during initial deployment.

faq.question: Can it help with Tailscale or reverse proxy access?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares those options, but recommends them only when private SSH access no longer fits the use case.

faq.question: What should users handle most carefully?
faq.answer: Users should review sudo commands, firewall changes, SSH hardening, service enablement, and gateway token output before applying guidance.
